title: Salisbury steak
subtitle: Seasoned beef patties in a rich onion and mushroom gravy
excerpt: Oval-shaped minced beef patties, seasoned and pan-fried, then finished in a deep brown onion and mushroom gravy. Not a hamburger and not a steak, its own thing, somewhere between the two. Served with mashed potato and the gravy poured generously over everything.
sourceNotes: Salisbury steak takes its name from Dr James Henry Salisbury (1823 to 1905), an American physician who prescribed minced beef as part of a meat-heavy diet he believed beneficial to health. It appears in American domestic cookery from the late nineteenth century and became a staple of American diner and home cooking in the mid-twentieth century, particularly through its appearance on TV dinner trays.

content.text: The patties are made from seasoned beef mince with a small amount of breadcrumbs and egg to bind them, the same principles as a meatloaf, but shaped oval and cooked in a pan rather than the oven. They are browned first, removed, and then the gravy is made in the same pan using the beef fat and browned bits left behind. The patties go back into the gravy to finish cooking, which means they absorb the flavour of the sauce and stay moist.

The gravy is the whole point. A proper brown onion gravy, dark and rich. Cook the onions until they are deep golden (this takes longer than most people expect, at least 12 minutes). Then build a simple beef stock sauce with a little Worcestershire sauce for depth. Serve with mashed potato so the gravy has somewhere to go.

Combine the {{beef-mince}} of beef mince, {{breadcrumbs-dried}} of breadcrumbs, {{eggs}} egg, {{dijon-mustard}} of Dijon mustard, 1 tsp {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire sauce, salt, and pepper in a bowl and mix until just combined. Divide into 4 portions and shape each into an oval patty about 2 cm thick. Heat a little oil in the frying pan over medium-high heat. Sear the patties for 3 minutes per side until deeply browned. Remove to a plate. They will not be cooked through at this stage.

Reduce the heat to medium and add the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter to the same pan. Add the {{onion}} slices and cook, stirring occasionally, for 12 to 15 minutes until deeply golden, do not rush this. Add the {{chestnut-mushrooms}} and cook for 4 minutes more until softened. Sprinkle over the {{plain-flour}} of plain flour and stir for 1 minute. Gradually pour in the {{stock-beef}} of beef stock and add the {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire sauce, scraping up the fond from the base. Bring to a simmer.
Finish the patties in the gravy

Return the patties to the pan, nestling them into the gravy. Simmer gently, uncovered, for 8 to 10 minutes, turning once, until cooked through. The gravy will thicken further as the patties cook. Serve with mashed potato, with the gravy ladled over.

items.fix: Ensure the egg and breadcrumbs are well incorporated; do not move the patties for the full 3 minutes, a crust that has formed will release cleanly
items.cause: Too little binder, or handled before the crust formed

items.fix: A proper brown onion gravy requires patience, 12 minutes minimum on medium heat; pale onions produce pale gravy
items.cause: Onions not cooked long enough; not enough caramelisation

items.fix: Check at 8 minutes, cut into one patty; the centre should be just cooked through with no pink remaining
Add a splash of beef stock or water and stir to loosen; the correct consistency coats the back of a spoon but still pours
items.cause: Too much flour, or reduced too far
items.symptom: Gravy is too thick and sticky

content.text: Salisbury steak is named after James Henry Salisbury, an American doctor who pushed minced beef as a health food in the late nineteenth century. He prescribed it to patients three times a day. The dish became a staple of American diner cooking and then of the frozen TV dinner, where Swanson's Salisbury steak tray was one of the most popular items from the 1950s onward. The link with frozen convenience food gave it an unfair reputation. The real thing, made with properly seasoned mince and a good gravy cooked with care, is a fine weeknight dinner that asks very little of the cook.
